sections.heading: Greedy Best First Search
sections.body: Greedy Best First Search expands the node that appears closest to the goal, using only the heuristic estimate h(n) to decide — it greedily chases whatever looks most promising right now, without considering how much it already cost to get there.

sections.heading: Algorithm
sections.body: Like UCS, Greedy Best First Search uses a priority queue for its frontier, but sorted by h(n) alone instead of cumulative path cost g(n) — the node estimated to be nearest the goal is always expanded next.

sections.heading: Evaluation Function
sections.body: Greedy Best First Search's evaluation function is simply f(n) = h(n) — it ignores the cost already incurred entirely, focusing purely on the estimated remaining distance.

sections.heading: Advantages
sections.body: Because it aggressively follows the heuristic toward the goal, Greedy Best First Search is often very fast in practice and can find a solution while exploring far fewer nodes than uninformed strategies, especially with a good heuristic.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.body: Greedy Best First Search is neither complete nor optimal in general — since it ignores path cost so far, it can get stuck in loops or dead ends chasing a locally attractive but ultimately misleading heuristic value, and even when it succeeds, the path found is often not the cheapest one.

quiz.question: Greedy Best First Search's evaluation function is:
What is the main limitation of Greedy Best First Search?
options: It always finds the optimal path
It is neither complete nor optimal in general, since it ignores cost already spent
It cannot use a priority queue
It requires no heuristic at all

quiz.question: Why can Greedy Best First Search be fast in practice?
options: It explores every node in the state space
It aggressively follows the heuristic, often exploring far fewer nodes than uninformed search
It always backtracks fully like DFS
